1.title: Glenna Collett Vare's era of amateur golf
1.text: American amateur great Glenna Collett Vare, often called the 'Female Bobby Jones,' was a six-time U.S. Women's Amateur champion whose dominance in the 1920s and 1930s shaped early women's golf; the LPGA's scoring award is named for her.

1.title: Cary Middlecoff wins the Masters in a romp
1.text: Cary Middlecoff posted a record seven-stroke victory at Augusta National, anchored by a famous 82-foot eagle putt on the 13th in the second round, to capture the 1955 Masters.

1.title: Future Open champion Justin Leonard born
1.text: American Justin Leonard, who won the 1997 Open Championship at Royal Troon and holed a memorable putt to clinch the 1999 Ryder Cup, was born on this day in Dallas, Texas.

1.title: Al Geiberger and the run to 'Mr. 59'
1.text: American Al Geiberger, born in 1937, was a steady PGA Tour winner whose lasting fame came weeks after this period when he became the first man to shoot 59 in a PGA Tour event at the 1977 Memphis Classic.

1.title: Sandy Lyle wins Greater Greensboro Open
1.text: Reigning Masters champion Sandy Lyle added the Kmart Greater Greensboro Open to his strong 1988 season with a victory in North Carolina.

1.title: Lucas Glover wins Wells Fargo Championship
1.text: Lucas Glover beat Jonathan Byrd in a playoff at Quail Hollow to win the Wells Fargo Championship, his first PGA Tour title since the 2009 U.S. Open.

2.title: Jock Hutchison wins the Open at St Andrews
2.text: Scottish-born American Jock Hutchison won the 1921 Open Championship at St Andrews in a playoff over amateur Roger Wethered, becoming the first U.S.-based player to take the Claret Jug.

2.title: Byron Nelson wins his first Masters
2.text: Byron Nelson made up six strokes on Ralph Guldahl over the 12th and 13th holes and went on to win his first Masters by two strokes at Augusta National.

2.title: Jack Nicklaus dominates with record Masters score
2.text: Jack Nicklaus shot a record 271 to win the 1965 Masters by nine strokes over Arnold Palmer and Gary Player, prompting Bobby Jones to remark that Nicklaus played a game with which he was not familiar.

2.title: Charles Coody wins the Masters
2.text: Charles Coody birdied two of the final three holes to overtake Jack Nicklaus and Johnny Miller and win the 1971 Masters by two strokes.

2.title: David Toms wins Compaq Classic
2.text: David Toms captured the Compaq Classic of New Orleans, one of the steady wins that built him toward his 2001 PGA Championship triumph.

2.title: Stephen Ames wins Western Open era event
2.text: Canada's Stephen Ames, who would later win THE PLAYERS in 2009, was among the rising international stars on the PGA Tour during this stretch of the early 2000s.

3.title: Future PGA champion Vic Ghezzi born
3.text: American Vic Ghezzi, who won the 1941 PGA Championship by defeating Byron Nelson in a 38-hole final, was born on this day in Rumson, New Jersey.

3.title: Ben Hogan begins his historic 1953 season
3.text: In 1953 Ben Hogan won five of the six tournaments he entered, including the Masters, U.S. Open and Open Championship, a 'Triple Crown' regarded as one of the greatest seasons in golf history.

3.title: Bruce Lietzke wins Byron Nelson Classic
3.text: Bruce Lietzke, known for his fade and his light schedule, won the Byron Nelson Golf Classic in Texas during a strong stretch of his career.

3.title: Brooks Koepka born in West Palm Beach
3.text: American Brooks Koepka, a five-time major champion including PGA Championships in 2018, 2019 and 2023, was born on this day in West Palm Beach, Florida.

3.title: Davis Love III wins Players-era event
3.text: Davis Love III, one of the most prolific PGA Tour winners of the 1990s, captured another title during a season that affirmed his place among the game's elite ball-strikers.

3.title: Rory McIlroy wins Wells Fargo Championship
3.text: Rory McIlroy closed with a 61 to win the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow by seven strokes, a course he has dominated throughout his career.

4.title: Macdonald Smith and the great Smith brothers
4.text: Macdonald Smith, born in Carnoustie, was one of three Scottish Smith brothers to win on tour; he captured 24 PGA Tour titles but famously never won a major despite numerous near misses.

4.title: Jimmy Demaret's Masters mastery
4.text: Jimmy Demaret became the first three-time Masters champion across his 1940, 1947 and 1950 victories, blending colorful style with a smooth swing that made him a fan favorite.

4.title: Jack Nicklaus wins thrilling 1975 Masters
4.text: Jack Nicklaus holed a 40-foot birdie putt on the 16th and held off Johnny Miller and Tom Weiskopf by one shot to win the 1975 Masters, widely regarded as one of the greatest ever played.

4.title: Tom Watson wins New Orleans event
4.text: Tom Watson, the dominant player of the era, added another PGA Tour title in 1980 on his way to a third straight money title and Player of the Year honors.

4.title: Rory McIlroy born in Northern Ireland
4.text: Rory McIlroy, a five-time major champion who completed the career Grand Slam at the 2025 Masters, was born on this day in Holywood, County Down, Northern Ireland.

4.title: J.B. Holmes wins Wells Fargo Championship
4.text: J.B. Holmes won the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow, completing a comeback after major neck surgery for an emotional return to the winner's circle.

5.title: Future major winner Dow Finsterwald born
5.text: American Dow Finsterwald, who won the 1958 PGA Championship in its first year of stroke play, was born on this day in Athens, Ohio.

5.title: Doug Ford wins the Masters
5.text: Doug Ford holed out from a greenside bunker on the 72nd hole to win the 1957 Masters by three strokes over Sam Snead.

5.title: Bob Goalby wins controversial Masters
5.text: Bob Goalby won the 1968 Masters after Roberto De Vicenzo signed for an incorrect scorecard that cost him a tie, leading to De Vicenzo's famous line, 'What a stupid I am.'

5.title: Curtis Strange wins in Houston
5.text: Curtis Strange, who topped the PGA Tour money list in 1985, captured another title that season as he established himself as one of America's best players.

5.title: Scott Hoch wins Greater Greensboro
5.text: Scott Hoch won the Greater Greensboro Chrysler Classic in North Carolina, one of his many PGA Tour titles spread across two decades.

5.title: Derek Ernst stuns field at Wells Fargo
5.text: Little-known Derek Ernst, who got into the field as an alternate, beat David Lynn in a playoff at Quail Hollow to win the Wells Fargo Championship for a shock first PGA Tour title.

6.title: Willie Macfarlane and the marathon U.S. Open
6.text: Scottish-born Willie Macfarlane won the 1925 U.S. Open by beating Bobby Jones in a 36-hole playoff after the two had tied across regulation, one of the era's most dramatic finishes.

6.title: Jack Burke Jr. wins the Masters
6.text: Jack Burke Jr. erased an eight-shot final-round deficit to Ken Venturi to win the 1956 Masters, the largest final-round comeback in the tournament's history at the time.

6.title: Arnold Palmer wins Masters playoff
6.text: Arnold Palmer shot 68 in an 18-hole playoff to beat Gary Player and Dow Finsterwald and capture his third Masters title in 1962.

6.title: Tommy Aaron wins the Masters
6.text: Tommy Aaron, who had once handed Roberto De Vicenzo the wrong scorecard at the 1968 Masters, won the 1973 Masters by one stroke for his lone major title.

6.title: Tom Kite wins Federal Express Classic
6.text: Tom Kite, the PGA Tour's all-time leading money winner at the time, claimed another title in 1990 during a career that culminated in the 1992 U.S. Open.

6.title: Rickie Fowler wins first PGA Tour title
6.text: Rickie Fowler beat Rory McIlroy and D.A. Points in a playoff at Quail Hollow to win the Wells Fargo Championship for his maiden PGA Tour victory.

7.title: Gene Sarazen wins first U.S. Open at age 20
7.text: In 1922 Gene Sarazen, then just 20, won the U.S. Open at Skokie, the first of seven majors that would make him one of only five players to complete the career Grand Slam.

7.title: Ralph Guldahl wins the Masters
7.text: Ralph Guldahl, already a two-time U.S. Open champion, birdied the 13th and eagled with a brilliant finish to win the 1939 Masters by one stroke over Sam Snead.

7.title: Gary Player becomes first foreign Masters champion
7.text: Gary Player won the 1961 Masters to become the first international winner of the tournament, edging Arnold Palmer after Palmer made double bogey on the 72nd hole.

7.title: Jack Nicklaus wins fourth Masters
7.text: Jack Nicklaus led wire-to-wire to win the 1972 Masters by three strokes, the fourth of his record six green jackets.

7.title: Tiger Woods rolls in his prime
7.text: During the spring of 2000 Tiger Woods was in the midst of a historic run that would soon produce the 'Tiger Slam' of four consecutive major titles held at once.

7.title: Seve Ballesteros dies at 54
7.text: Spanish legend Seve Ballesteros, a five-time major champion and Ryder Cup icon whose creativity and charisma transformed European golf, died on this day at his home in Pedreña after a battle with brain cancer.

8.title: Henry Picard wins the Masters
8.text: Henry Picard played the final 18 holes in dominant fashion to win the 1938 Masters by two strokes over Ralph Guldahl and Harry Cooper.

8.title: Sam Snead wins first Masters
8.text: Sam Snead closed with rounds of 67-67 to win the 1949 Masters by three strokes, the year the green jacket was first awarded to the champion.

8.title: Arnold Palmer's charge wins the Masters
8.text: Arnold Palmer birdied the final two holes to beat Ken Venturi by one and win the 1960 Masters, a finish that helped define his go-for-broke legend.

8.title: Don January wins event during banner year
8.text: Don January, who would win the 1967 PGA Championship the following year, was a steady winner on the PGA Tour and later a star on the senior circuit.

8.title: Tom Watson wins late-career title
8.text: Tom Watson, long after his major-championship heyday, remained a respected competitor and would capture the 1998 Colonial during his enduring PGA Tour career.

8.title: James Hahn wins Wells Fargo Championship
8.text: James Hahn beat Roberto Castro in a playoff to win the Wells Fargo Championship, played at Eagle Point in Wilmington while Quail Hollow prepared to host the PGA Championship.

9.title: Future major winner Dave Marr born
9.text: American Dave Marr, who won the 1965 PGA Championship and later became a beloved television golf commentator, was born on this day in Houston, Texas.

9.title: Sam Snead wins Masters playoff over Hogan
9.text: Sam Snead shot 70 to Ben Hogan's 71 in an 18-hole playoff to win the 1954 Masters, one of the most celebrated head-to-head duels between the two rivals.

9.title: Future world No. 1 born this era
9.text: The mid-1960s produced a generation of stars who would carry professional golf into its television-driven boom, building on the popularity of Palmer, Nicklaus and Player.

9.title: Craig Stadler wins the Masters
9.text: Craig 'The Walrus' Stadler held off Dan Pohl in a playoff to win the 1982 Masters for the biggest title of his career.

9.title: Nick Price wins during his peak run
9.text: Nick Price, who would reach World No. 1 the following year, was in the midst of a dominant stretch that produced three major championships in the early-to-mid 1990s.

9.title: Rory McIlroy wins first PGA Tour title at Quail Hollow
9.text: At age 20, Rory McIlroy closed with a course-record 62 to win the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow by four strokes for his first PGA Tour victory.

10.title: Macdonald Smith's near misses
10.text: Macdonald Smith, one of the finest players never to win a major, contended in numerous U.S. and British Opens during the 1920s and 1930s while winning two dozen PGA Tour events.

10.title: Ben Hogan wins second Masters
10.text: Ben Hogan shot a record 274 to win the 1953 Masters by five strokes, the opening leg of his celebrated three-major season.

10.title: Art Wall Jr. wins the Masters
10.text: Art Wall Jr. birdied five of the last six holes to charge from behind and win the 1959 Masters by one stroke.

10.title: Billy Casper wins Masters playoff
10.text: Billy Casper shot 69 in an 18-hole playoff to beat Gene Littler and win the 1970 Masters, the last of his three major titles.

10.title: Fred Couples wins Memorial during big year
10.text: Fred Couples, fresh off his 1992 Masters win and World No. 1 ranking, continued a banner season that established him as the most popular player in the game.

10.title: Sean O'Hair wins Quail Hollow Championship
10.text: Sean O'Hair held off Tiger Woods and Kevin Stadler to win the Quail Hollow Championship in Charlotte for one of his PGA Tour titles.

11.title: Cyril Walker wins the U.S. Open
11.text: Englishman Cyril Walker, a slender and deliberate player, won the 1924 U.S. Open at Oakland Hills by three strokes over Bobby Jones for his lone major title.

11.title: Arnold Palmer wins first Masters
11.text: Arnold Palmer won the 1958 Masters, the first of his four green jackets and the breakthrough major that helped ignite golf's television boom.

11.title: George Archer wins the Masters
11.text: George Archer, one of the best putters of his era, won the 1969 Masters by one stroke over a trio of challengers for his only major title.

11.title: Bob Tway's rise to the PGA Championship
11.text: Bob Tway, who holed a famous bunker shot to beat Greg Norman at the 1986 PGA Championship, was enjoying a breakout year that earned him PGA Tour Player of the Year honors.

11.title: Greg Norman wins in his veteran years
11.text: Greg Norman, the longtime World No. 1, remained a force in the late 1990s, adding to a career total of more than 90 worldwide professional victories.

11.title: Anthony Kim wins Quail Hollow Championship
11.text: Anthony Kim, one of the most promising young Americans of his era, won the Quail Hollow Championship by two strokes in Charlotte for an early-career PGA Tour title.

12.title: Jock Hutchison era begins
12.text: Jock Hutchison, born in St Andrews, emigrated to the United States and became one of the first naturalized American stars, winning the 1920 PGA Championship and the 1921 Open.

12.title: Herman Keiser wins the Masters
12.text: Herman Keiser, a longshot known as the 'Missouri Mortician,' held on to beat Ben Hogan by one stroke to win the 1946 Masters.

12.title: Mike Souchak and the scoring barrage
12.text: Mike Souchak, who set a 72-hole PGA Tour scoring record of 257 at the 1955 Texas Open, was among the longest hitters and lowest scorers of the 1950s.

12.title: Gary Player wins second Masters
12.text: Gary Player won the 1974 Masters by two strokes, the second of his three green jackets and part of a career that produced nine major titles.

12.title: Ian Woosnam wins the Masters
12.text: Welshman Ian Woosnam parred the 72nd hole to win the 1991 Masters by one stroke and briefly hold the World No. 1 ranking.

12.title: Max Homa wins Wells Fargo Championship
12.text: Max Homa, who had lost his PGA Tour card a year earlier, won the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow by three strokes for his breakthrough first PGA Tour title.

13.title: Future Masters host Bobby Jones era
13.text: Bobby Jones, the amateur who would win 13 majors and the 1930 Grand Slam, co-founded Augusta National and the Masters Tournament, shaping golf's most famous event.

13.title: Ken Venturi's near-Masters as an amateur
13.text: Amateur Ken Venturi led the 1956 Masters entering the final round before a closing 80 cost him the title; he would later win the 1964 U.S. Open in brutal heat.

13.title: Fuzzy Zoeller wins the Masters as a rookie
13.text: Fuzzy Zoeller won the 1979 Masters in the tournament's first sudden-death playoff, beating Ed Sneed and Tom Watson to become the first to win in his Masters debut since 1935.

13.title: Nick Faldo's repeat at Augusta
13.text: Nick Faldo won the 1990 Masters in a playoff over Raymond Floyd to become the first to successfully defend the title since Jack Nicklaus, part of a run that made him World No. 1.

13.title: David Toms wins Compaq Classic
13.text: David Toms won the Compaq Classic of New Orleans, a victory in the same season he would capture his lone major at the PGA Championship.

13.title: Jason Day wins Wells Fargo Championship
13.text: Jason Day held off Nick Watney and Aaron Wise to win the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow for one of his many PGA Tour titles.

14.title: Horton Smith wins second Masters
14.text: Horton Smith, who had won the inaugural 1934 Masters, captured his second green jacket in 1936 with a one-stroke victory at Augusta National.

14.title: Future major winner Tony Lema remembered
14.text: Tony 'Champagne' Lema, winner of the 1964 Open Championship, died young in a 1966 plane crash; his charisma and clutch play made him one of the most popular players of the early 1960s.

14.title: Gary Player's final-round 64 wins Masters
14.text: Gary Player closed with a 64, birdieing seven of the last 10 holes, to come from seven back and win the 1978 Masters, the last of his nine major titles.

14.title: Nick Faldo wins first Masters in playoff
14.text: Nick Faldo birdied the 11th in a sudden-death playoff to beat Scott Hoch and win his first Masters in 1989.

14.title: Tiger Woods wins Byron Nelson Classic
14.text: Tiger Woods won the GTE Byron Nelson Classic in Texas during his historic 2000 season, in which he captured three of the four major championships.

14.title: Brian Harman wins Wells Fargo Championship
14.text: Brian Harman birdied the 72nd hole to beat Dustin Johnson and Pat Perez by one and win the Wells Fargo Championship at Eagle Point.

15.title: Fred Herd wins the U.S. Open
15.text: Scottish-born Fred Herd won the 1898 U.S. Open at Myopia Hunt Club, the first U.S. Open contested over 72 holes; he reportedly had to leave a deposit on the trophy.

15.title: Cary Middlecoff wins first U.S. Open
15.text: Cary Middlecoff, a former dentist, won the 1949 U.S. Open at Medinah by one stroke over Sam Snead and Clayton Heafner for the first of his two U.S. Open titles.

15.title: Al Geiberger shoots historic 59
15.text: Al Geiberger became the first player to shoot 59 in a PGA Tour event, doing so in the second round of the Danny Thomas Memphis Classic at Colonial Country Club.

15.title: Sandy Lyle's strong Masters year
15.text: Sandy Lyle, who in April 1988 became the first British winner of the Masters with a clutch birdie from a fairway bunker on the 72nd hole, continued a standout season.

15.title: Sergio Garcia wins Wachovia Championship
15.text: Sergio Garcia beat Jim Furyk and Padraig Harrington in a playoff to win the Wachovia Championship at Quail Hollow, an early signature win in his career.

15.title: Max Homa defends Wells Fargo title
15.text: Max Homa won the Wells Fargo Championship at TPC Potomac, his second Wells Fargo title, while Quail Hollow was unavailable due to the Presidents Cup preparations.

16.title: Mungo Park and the early Open champions
16.text: The Park family of Musselburgh, including Open champions Willie Park Sr. and Mungo Park, were among the dominant figures of golf's earliest professional era in Scotland.

16.title: Ben Hogan wins first major in years
16.text: Ben Hogan won the 1948 PGA Championship and 1948 U.S. Open in a season of dominance, the year before the car accident that nearly ended his life and career.

16.title: Lee Trevino's golden summer approaches
16.text: Lee Trevino, in 1971, would go on to win the U.S. Open, Canadian Open and Open Championship within a few weeks, one of the great stretches in golf history.

16.title: Raymond Floyd wins Memorial Tournament
16.text: Raymond Floyd, a four-time major champion, won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, continuing a long and decorated PGA Tour career.

16.title: David Duval reaches World No. 1 era
16.text: David Duval, who shot 59 in the final round to win the 1999 Bob Hope Classic, ascended to World No. 1 in 1999 during a brilliant peak before injuries slowed him.

16.title: Rory McIlroy wins Wells Fargo Championship
16.text: Rory McIlroy closed with a 68 to win the Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow by one stroke, ending an 18-month winless drought.

17.title: Willie Anderson's U.S. Open run
17.text: Willie Anderson, the only man to win three consecutive U.S. Opens (1903-1905), captured four titles in all and was among the most dominant players of golf's early American era.

17.title: Ben Hogan begins Masters-to-Open triple
17.text: In 1953, weeks after winning the Masters, Ben Hogan went on to claim the U.S. Open and his only Open Championship, completing a legendary three-major season.

17.title: David Graham wins on PGA Tour
17.text: Australian David Graham, who won the 1979 PGA Championship and 1981 U.S. Open, was among the most accomplished international players of his generation.

17.title: David Toms born era of Tour stars
17.text: The early 1990s saw the emergence of a wave of American professionals who would dominate the PGA Tour into the new century alongside Tiger Woods.

17.title: Angel Cabrera defends amid major form
17.text: Angel Cabrera, who won the 2007 U.S. Open and the 2009 Masters, was one of the most powerful and resilient players of his era, a two-time major champion from Argentina.

17.title: Rory McIlroy wins second Wells Fargo title
17.text: Rory McIlroy closed with a course-record 61 to win the 2015 Wells Fargo Championship at Quail Hollow by seven strokes, one of his most dominant performances there.

18.title: Ralph Guldahl's U.S. Open dominance
18.text: Ralph Guldahl won back-to-back U.S. Opens in 1937 and 1938 and the 1939 Masters, a three-year stretch as one of the best players in the world before a sudden loss of form.

18.title: Dow Finsterwald wins first stroke-play PGA
18.text: Dow Finsterwald won the 1958 PGA Championship, the first year the event switched from match play to stroke play, holding off Billy Casper by two strokes.

18.title: Tom Watson's career takes off
18.text: Tom Watson won his first major at the 1975 Open Championship at Carnoustie, the beginning of a Hall of Fame career that produced eight major titles.

18.title: Davis Love III wins his only major
18.text: Davis Love III won the 1997 PGA Championship at Winged Foot by five strokes, finishing under a rainbow as he completed his lone major title.

18.title: Adam Scott reaches World No. 1
18.text: Adam Scott, the 2013 Masters champion, ascended to World No. 1 for the first time in May 2014, becoming the first Australian to top the men's rankings.

18.title: Scottie Scheffler wins PGA Championship at Quail Hollow
18.text: Scottie Scheffler shot 11-under 273 to win the 2025 PGA Championship by five strokes at Quail Hollow, capturing his third major title and first Wanamaker Trophy.

19.title: Harry Vardon's American tour
19.text: Harry Vardon, six-time Open champion and member of the 'Great Triumvirate,' won the 1900 U.S. Open during a celebrated barnstorming tour that helped popularize golf in America.

19.title: Julius Boros wins PGA as oldest major champ
19.text: At 48, Julius Boros won the 1968 PGA Championship at Pecan Valley to become the oldest major champion, a record that stood until Phil Mickelson in 2021.

19.title: Hubert Green wins PGA Championship
19.text: Hubert Green held off Lee Trevino by two strokes to win the 1985 PGA Championship at Cherry Hills, the second major title of his career.

19.title: Rich Beem stuns the field
19.text: Rich Beem, a former cellphone salesman, would go on to win the 2002 PGA Championship by holding off a charging Tiger Woods, one of the era's most surprising major winners.

19.title: Brooks Koepka defends PGA at Bethpage Black
19.text: Brooks Koepka withstood a back-nine charge from Dustin Johnson to win the 2019 PGA Championship at Bethpage Black by two strokes, his fourth major and second straight PGA title.

19.title: Xander Schauffele wins PGA with record score
19.text: Xander Schauffele birdied the 72nd hole to win the 2024 PGA Championship at Valhalla at 21-under 263, the lowest 72-hole score in major championship history, for his first major.

20.title: Walter Hagen's professional pride
20.text: Walter Hagen, an 11-time major champion and the first American-born U.S. Open winner, did more than anyone to elevate the status of the touring professional in the 1920s.

20.title: Gene Littler wins the U.S. Open
20.text: Gene Littler, known for his elegant swing, won the 1962 U.S. Open at Oakland Hills, the lone major title of his Hall of Fame career.

20.title: Hale Irwin wins second U.S. Open
20.text: Hale Irwin won the 1979 U.S. Open at Inverness, the second of his three U.S. Open titles spanning from 1974 to 1990.

20.title: Wayne Levi's hot streak
20.text: Wayne Levi won four PGA Tour titles in 1990 and was named PGA Tour Player of the Year, an unusual honor that year over players who had won majors.

20.title: Tiger Woods wins Wachovia Championship
20.text: Tiger Woods won the Wachovia Championship at Quail Hollow by two strokes, one of his victories at a course that would later host the PGA Championship.

20.title: Aaron Wise wins AT&T Byron Nelson
20.text: Aaron Wise ran away with the AT&T Byron Nelson by three strokes at Trinity Forest in Dallas for his first PGA Tour victory.

21.title: Jim Barnes wins the U.S. Open
21.text: Jim Barnes won the 1921 U.S. Open at Columbia by nine strokes, a dominant victory that added to his 1916 and 1919 PGA Championship titles.

21.title: Jerry Barber wins PGA Championship
21.text: Jerry Barber holed three long putts on the final holes to force a playoff, then beat Don January to win the 1961 PGA Championship at age 45.

21.title: Tom Kite's strong season
21.text: Tom Kite was named PGA Tour Player of the Year and topped the money list in 1989, a season that cemented his standing among the best players never to have won a major at that point.

21.title: Future stars of the modern era
21.text: The mid-1990s set the stage for golf's globalization, with international players from Europe, Australia, South Africa and Asia increasingly contending at the highest level.

21.title: Billy Horschel wins AT&T Byron Nelson
21.text: Billy Horschel beat Jason Day in a playoff to win the AT&T Byron Nelson, his first PGA Tour win in nearly three years.

21.title: Brooks Koepka wins third PGA Championship
21.text: Brooks Koepka shot a closing 67 to win the 2023 PGA Championship at Oak Hill by two strokes, his fifth major title and third PGA, completing a comeback from years of injury.

22.title: Tommy Armour wins the U.S. Open
22.text: Tommy Armour, the 'Silver Scot,' won the 1927 U.S. Open at Oakmont in a playoff over Harry Cooper for the first of his three major titles.

22.title: Doug Ford wins PGA Championship
22.text: Doug Ford defeated Cary Middlecoff in the final to win the 1955 PGA Championship, then still contested at match play.

22.title: Al Geiberger's record-setting week
22.text: Days after shooting his historic 59, Al Geiberger completed the win at the 1977 Danny Thomas Memphis Classic, cementing his nickname 'Mr. 59.'

22.title: Nick Price's march to World No. 1
22.text: Nick Price won the 1994 Open Championship and PGA Championship that summer, a dominant season that lifted the Zimbabwean to World No. 1.

22.title: Sergio Garcia wins AT&T Byron Nelson
22.text: Sergio Garcia beat Brooks Koepka in a playoff to win the AT&T Byron Nelson at TPC Four Seasons in Texas.

22.title: Justin Thomas wins PGA Championship in playoff
22.text: Justin Thomas rallied from seven strokes back, tying the largest comeback in PGA Championship history, and beat Will Zalatoris in a three-hole playoff at Southern Hills for his second major.

23.title: Willie Macfarlane's playoff classic
23.text: Willie Macfarlane outlasted Bobby Jones in a 36-hole playoff to win the 1925 U.S. Open at Worcester, one of the closest finishes of the decade.

23.title: Ben Hogan wins PGA Championship
23.text: Ben Hogan won the 1948 PGA Championship at Norwood Hills, defeating Mike Turnesa in the final during one of the best seasons of his career.

23.title: Al Geiberger and the Tour's stars
23.text: By the mid-1970s the PGA Tour featured deep fields led by Jack Nicklaus, Tom Watson, Johnny Miller and Hubert Green, ushering in a competitive new era.

23.title: David Duval's banner year
23.text: David Duval, who reached World No. 1 in 1999, won multiple times that season and would capture his lone major at the 2001 Open Championship.

23.title: Sam Snead dies at 89
23.text: Sam Snead, holder of the record 82 PGA Tour victories and a three-time Masters champion famed for his smooth swing, died on this day in Hot Springs, Virginia, four days shy of his 90th birthday.

23.title: Phil Mickelson becomes oldest major champion
23.text: At 50, Phil Mickelson won the 2021 PGA Championship at Kiawah Island's Ocean Course by two strokes to become the oldest major champion in golf history.

24.title: Billy Burke wins marathon U.S. Open
24.text: Billy Burke won the 1931 U.S. Open after a 72-hole playoff with George Von Elm, the longest playoff in major championship history, finally prevailing at Inverness.

24.title: Bob Rosburg wins PGA Championship
24.text: Bob Rosburg, one of the era's finest putters, charged from behind to win the 1959 PGA Championship at Minneapolis by one stroke.

24.title: Larry Nelson wins first PGA Championship
24.text: Larry Nelson, who took up golf only in his 20s, won the 1981 PGA Championship at Atlanta Athletic Club by four strokes for his first major title.

24.title: Fred Couples at his peak
24.text: Fred Couples, the 1992 Masters champion and reigning World No. 1, was at the height of his powers, beloved for his languid swing and easygoing manner.

24.title: David Toms wins Colonial
24.text: David Toms birdied the 72nd hole to beat Tim Clark by one and win the Crowne Plaza Invitational at Colonial in Fort Worth, a course that suited his precise game.

24.title: Steven Bowditch wins AT&T Byron Nelson
24.text: Australia's Steven Bowditch held off the field to win the AT&T Byron Nelson in Texas for his second PGA Tour title.

25.title: Cyril Walker's surprise U.S. Open
25.text: Cyril Walker, a transplanted Englishman, methodically picked his way to the 1924 U.S. Open title at Oakland Hills, three strokes clear of the great Bobby Jones.

25.title: Mickey Wright's golden era begins
25.text: Mickey Wright, regarded by many as the greatest woman golfer in history, won 82 LPGA titles including 13 majors and at one point held all four LPGA major titles at once.

25.title: Raymond Floyd wins first PGA Championship
25.text: Raymond Floyd led wire-to-wire to win the 1969 PGA Championship at NCR Country Club in Dayton for his first major title.

25.title: Bob Tway's breakout season
25.text: Bob Tway, who would win the PGA Championship that August with a holed bunker shot, was in the midst of a four-win season that earned him PGA Tour Player of the Year.

25.title: Kenny Perry wins Colonial in record style
25.text: Kenny Perry birdied the final hole to win the Bank of America Colonial at Fort Worth, one of three wins in a strong 2003 season.

25.title: Brendon Todd wins HP Byron Nelson
25.text: Brendon Todd held on to win the HP Byron Nelson Championship in Texas by two strokes for his first PGA Tour victory.

26.title: Bobby Jones and the road to the Grand Slam
26.text: Bobby Jones, the greatest amateur in golf history, won the 1929 U.S. Open at Winged Foot in a playoff, a stepping stone toward his unmatched 1930 Grand Slam.

26.title: Lionel Hebert wins PGA Championship
26.text: Lionel Hebert won the 1957 PGA Championship, the last to be contested at match play, defeating Dow Finsterwald in the final.

26.title: Lee Trevino's emergence
26.text: Lee Trevino burst onto the scene by winning the 1968 U.S. Open at Oak Hill with four sub-70 rounds, launching a Hall of Fame career and six major titles.

26.title: Tom Purtzer wins Colonial
26.text: Tom Purtzer, owner of one of the era's most admired swings, won the Southwestern Bell Colonial in Fort Worth, one of his PGA Tour titles.

26.title: Nick Price wins late-career title
26.text: Nick Price, a former World No. 1 and three-time major champion, remained competitive into the 2000s while compiling 18 PGA Tour victories.

26.title: Boo Weekley wins Colonial
26.text: Boo Weekley closed with a 64 to win the Crowne Plaza Invitational at Colonial in Fort Worth, ending a five-year PGA Tour victory drought.

27.title: Sam Snead born in Virginia
27.text: Sam Snead, winner of a record 82 PGA Tour titles and seven majors, famed for his fluid swing and longevity, was born on this day in Ashwood, Virginia.

27.title: Bobby Jones nears his breakthrough
27.text: Bobby Jones broke through for his first major at the 1923 U.S. Open, beginning a seven-year run in which he won 13 majors and retired at 28 after the 1930 Grand Slam.

27.title: Jack Nicklaus turns pro and conquers
27.text: Jack Nicklaus, in his rookie pro season of 1962, beat Arnold Palmer in a playoff to win the U.S. Open at Oakmont, the first of his record 18 major titles.

27.title: Peter Jacobsen wins Colonial
27.text: Peter Jacobsen won the Colonial National Invitation in Fort Worth, one of the popular American's PGA Tour victories.

27.title: Rory Sabbatini wins Colonial over Tiger
27.text: Rory Sabbatini, who had publicly criticized Tiger Woods, beat him head-to-head down the stretch to win the Colonial in Fort Worth.

27.title: Justin Rose wins Fort Worth Invitational
27.text: England's Justin Rose beat Brooks Koepka in a playoff to win the Fort Worth Invitational at Colonial, a victory that returned him near the top of the world rankings.

28.title: Willie Smith wins U.S. Open by 11
28.text: Scottish-born Willie Smith won the 1899 U.S. Open at Baltimore by 11 strokes, a record margin of victory that stood for decades.

28.title: Jack Burke Jr. wins PGA Championship
28.text: Jack Burke Jr. capped a remarkable 1956 by winning the PGA Championship at Blue Hill, adding to his Masters title earlier that spring.

28.title: Tom Watson wins Byron Nelson Classic
28.text: Tom Watson, the dominant player of the late 1970s, won the Byron Nelson Golf Classic in Texas during a stretch of multiple Player of the Year awards.

28.title: Tom Lehman wins Colonial
28.text: Tom Lehman, who would win the 1996 Open Championship, captured the Colonial National Invitation in Fort Worth for a key PGA Tour title.

28.title: Phil Mickelson wins Colonial
28.text: Phil Mickelson won the MasterCard Colonial in Fort Worth, one of his many PGA Tour titles during a decorated career that produced six major championships.

28.title: Kevin Kisner wins Colonial
28.text: Kevin Kisner closed with a 67 to win the Dean & DeLuca Invitational at Colonial by one stroke for his second PGA Tour title.

29.title: Horace Rawlins and the first U.S. Open
29.text: The U.S. Open began in 1895, with Englishman Horace Rawlins winning the inaugural championship at Newport at age 21, launching one of golf's grandest traditions.

29.title: Sam Snead wins PGA Championship
29.text: Sam Snead won the 1949 PGA Championship, adding to his Masters title that spring in one of the standout seasons of his Hall of Fame career.

29.title: Tom Watson's run continues
29.text: Tom Watson, who won both the Masters and the Open Championship in 1977, including his famous 'Duel in the Sun' with Jack Nicklaus, was the game's hottest player.

29.title: Lanny Wadkins wins Colonial
29.text: Lanny Wadkins, a fierce competitor and 1977 PGA champion, won the Colonial National Invitation in Fort Worth during his standout PGA Tour career.

29.title: David Toms wins Colonial
29.text: David Toms birdied the 18th to beat Charlie Wi in a playoff and win the Crowne Plaza Invitational at Colonial in Fort Worth.

29.title: Jordan Spieth wins Colonial
29.text: Hometown favorite Jordan Spieth, a Dallas native, won the Dean & DeLuca Invitational at Colonial in Fort Worth for one of his early PGA Tour titles.

30.title: Horace Rawlins wins inaugural U.S. Open era
30.text: The early U.S. Opens of the 1890s, won by transplanted British professionals like Horace Rawlins and Joe Lloyd, helped establish championship golf in the United States.

30.title: Ralph Guldahl wins first U.S. Open
30.text: Ralph Guldahl won the 1937 U.S. Open at Oakland Hills, setting a then-record 72-hole score to begin his brief but brilliant run of dominance.

30.title: Gary Player wins U.S. Open for career Grand Slam
30.text: Gary Player won the 1965 U.S. Open at Bellerive in a playoff over Kel Nagle, becoming the third player to complete the career Grand Slam.

30.title: Tom Watson wins Memorial era
30.text: Tom Watson, weeks before his iconic chip-in to win the 1982 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach, was at the height of a career that produced eight major championships.

30.title: Olin Browne wins his first title
30.text: Olin Browne, a late bloomer who would later win the U.S. Senior Open, earned an early PGA Tour victory during a long and resilient career.

30.title: Justin Rose wins Memorial Tournament
30.text: England's Justin Rose won the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village by three strokes for one of his early PGA Tour titles.

31.title: Joe Lloyd wins the U.S. Open
31.text: Englishman Joe Lloyd eagled the final hole to win the 1897 U.S. Open at Chicago Golf Club by one stroke, an early dramatic finish in the championship's history.

31.title: Lew Worsham wins U.S. Open playoff
31.text: Lew Worsham beat Sam Snead by one stroke in an 18-hole playoff to win the 1947 U.S. Open at St. Louis after a famously contested final putt.

31.title: Tony Jacklin's transatlantic triumphs
31.text: Englishman Tony Jacklin, the 1969 Open champion, won the 1970 U.S. Open at Hazeltine by seven strokes, becoming the first British player to hold both Open titles at once in decades.

31.title: Curtis Strange's dominant year
31.text: Curtis Strange led the PGA Tour money list in 1987 and would go on to win back-to-back U.S. Opens in 1988 and 1989, the first to do so since Ben Hogan.

31.title: Davis Love III wins Kmart Greater Greensboro
31.text: Davis Love III won the Kmart Greater Greensboro Open, one of his many PGA Tour titles during a Hall of Fame career.

31.title: Justin Rose wins Memorial Tournament
31.text: Justin Rose closed strong to win the Memorial Tournament at Muirfield Village, his second Memorial title and one of several wins during his prime years.
